chapters.title: Studying a life helps predict how people will act
chapters.text:
Picture a world without violence, where someone had stopped Hitler and prevented 9/11. That world is not possible, but we can dig into the motives and forces that drive behavior and use what we find to ease tension and build something better for the next generation. By historical standards, human violence has actually dropped, and the neuroscientist Robert Sapolsky sees real reason for hope.

Sapolsky walks the reader through a full analysis of human behavior, backed by field observations and research in neuroscience, biology, behaviorism, ethology, and the social sciences. For newcomers to the field, the topics build gradually, layer on layer.

In Sapolsky's words, we hate and fear the wrong kind of violence, the kind that shows up in the wrong setting. Violence in the right setting is something else. We happily pay to watch it in a stadium, and we teach our kids to stand up for themselves.

This summary zooms in on one piece of the picture and traces the threads and patterns that shape how people act. Starting with the structure of the brain and what each part does, we move on to the usual suspects in hormone lore, the stories that need busting, and then into what is happening with toddlers inside and outside the womb to ask whether it is possible to cure someone of being bad.

Is free will real, or is every choice just the next thing triggered in a chain? If the second view has merit, the justice system might have to change, perhaps by weaving in more science. While the summary hands you theory, it also invites you to look at your own cultural and personal background and how it shapes who you are today. Knowing the universal rules makes decisions easier, helps you get what you want at work and at home, and lets you build relationships where both sides win.

Brain size varies from one adult to the next, and there is a correlation with intelligence. So yes, in this one case, size matters.

chapters.title: The brain is built in layers, each one adding something
chapters.text:
To make sense of behavior, you have to look at the brain at a stack of levels. The nervous system, sensory input, and hormones are all part of the picture. You can also keep going back, millions of years into our evolutionary history. This summary sticks to the pieces that matter most for our best and worst moments, and it starts with the basic question of why a behavior happened in the first place.

We move backward in time, step by step, from the moment of action, then back through the last few seconds, and then out to years ago. Each time window maps onto a different slice of neuroscience. So what was happening one second before the action? That is where neurobiology comes in, the field that studies the brain and the job each region does.

The neuroscientist Paul MacLean proposed that the brain has three main layers stacked on top of each other.

The oldest layer of the brain runs the body's most basic systems, like hunger and temperature. The next layer, the limbic system, came along later in evolution. It takes in emotional signals and pushes commands back down to the older layer, for example ordering a stress response when you see a lion, with the familiar options of freeze, run, or fight. The cortex is the youngest layer, the one that handles memory, abstraction, philosophy, and higher thought. It takes information from the outside world, sends emotional reactions down to the limbic system, and then influences the oldest layer.

Emotions shape what we remember and how we remember it.

The limbic system and the cortex are wired together, and they turn each other on and off in a kind of conversation that coordinates the whole organism.

The amygdala sits inside the limbic system and is heavily involved in reactions to threat and fear, as well as in aggression. Experiments show that you can spark rage in a person by stimulating the right neurons, or even by showing them pictures that stir anger. What happens when the amygdala is damaged by disease or surgery? Could aggression be dialed down by removing or quieting that part of the brain? Those questions run straight into bioethics, and the short answer is that it is possible in theory, but only about a third of the documented cases saw real improvement, so this kind of surgery is no longer standard practice.

The amygdala is also tied to anxiety, to fear that is innate or learned, and even to sexual motivation in men. Aggression and fear are only loosely connected. Fear does not always lead to aggression, but it cranks up the dial in people who were already prone to it, including people with psychopathic traits.

chapters.title: Sensory input quietly shapes how we act
chapters.text:
Now that we have covered the immediate brain events, it is time to widen the window. What was going on seconds to minutes before the action?

Ethology and behaviorism both study behavior, but they take very different paths. Behaviorists focus on universal stimulus and response loops, while ethologists dig into the variety of species-specific demands and behaviors. Looking at how animals behave helps us spot the sensory triggers that matter, whether those triggers are sounds, sights, or signals in a species' own language. The limbic system picks up those signals and sets off a response. The catch is that the cue is often below the surface, and we are not consciously aware of it. For example, people rate a more expensive placebo painkiller as more effective. People rate neutral faces from another race as angrier than faces from their own race. People are more likely to hire or vote for someone they find attractive.

Outside information shapes behavior, but the body's internal state matters too. The relationship is not as simple as pain or hunger making everyone more aggressive. Pain actually pushes naturally aggressive people toward more aggression and naturally nonaggressive people toward less. In one case the dial goes up, in the other it goes down.

Some of the cues are even more delicate, especially in group settings. Men, for instance, take more risks and act more aggressively, and they spend more on luxury items, when women are around or when women are on their mind. Another example comes from James Q. Wilson and George Kelling, the scholars behind the broken window theory. The idea is that trash, graffiti, and broken windows signal urban disorder, and that signal invites more crime. Once people see a norm being broken, they become more willing to break other norms, sometimes on purpose, sometimes because they were already inclined to.

Try not to make big decisions when you are angry or stressed. Those states cloud your thinking, and emotions can push you toward the wrong call.

The brain also reorders its own sensitivity. When you are hungry, for example, the smell of food jumps to the front of your attention, because the brain tells your sensory organs to lean in.

The takeaway is that behavior is not as rational or deliberate as we like to think. It is steered by signals we do not consciously register from the outside world and by the state of the body, both of which unfold in the seconds and minutes before we decide to act.

chapters.title: Testosterone feels good because it lifts confidence
chapters.text:
Now we move from the brain to the hormones. What was happening hours to days before the action? Looking at the two ends of the hormone spectrum, testosterone is often linked to aggression, while oxytocin is often linked to comfort. The stereotype is that men are more aggressive and violent than women, and one reason is that men carry more testosterone. So the hormone gets tied to aggression. But does testosterone actually cause aggression?

Experiments show that male aggression can persist even after castration, which removes most of the body's testosterone. Prior aggression levels also matter, since a more aggressive man before surgery is likely to remain more aggressive afterward. The relationship actually runs the other way too, since being aggressive drives testosterone higher. Aggression seems to come more from social learning than from hormones, while testosterone lifts optimism and self-confidence and lowers anxiety and fear. The downside is that it can make a person cockier, more selfish, and more impulsive. Winning a challenge, or watching your team win, pushes testosterone up and reinforces the sense of being on top.

Oxytocin, by contrast, has a friendlier reputation. It calms aggression and makes other people seem more trustworthy. It also drives the bond between a mother and her child, lighting up the so-called maternal instinct. Oxytocin helps couples stay monogamous, since nature rewards sticking with one partner with a stronger hormonal hit than moving on to someone new.

Time spent with a dog raises oxytocin levels in both the dog and the owner.

There is a darker side, though. Oxytocin does make us more social and generous, but only toward people we see as part of our group, the Us. Toward the people we see as Them, oxytocin can flip the script and make us treat them more like enemies. Other examples include maternal aggression when offspring are at risk and the kind of female-on-female competition that shows up across many species. The honest summary is that hormones do not write our responses, but they make us more reactive to social triggers and they amplify the tendencies we already carry.

A quick cultural note. PMS, premenstrual syndrome, looks different across cultures. In China, for example, women tend to report milder symptoms than women in the United States.

chapters.title: Adolescents are more emotional than children and adults
chapters.text:
After working through the activity inside the brain, we can go all the way back to childhood. The brain goes through several stages of development, and that development keeps going until the end of adolescence. Jean Piaget, the famous developmental psychologist, broke it into at least four major phases.

In the sensorimotor stage, which runs up to about age two, infants explore the world and start sensing the objects around them. After eight months, they can even imagine things that are not in front of them. The preoperational stage, up to about age seven, brings more symbolic thinking, but kids still cannot reason logically or grasp cause and effect. The concrete operational stage, up to about age twelve, is when children start thinking more logically, although they can still stumble in situations where literal thinking trips them up. The formal operational stage, which starts in adolescence, is when metacognition and abstract thinking really come online.

Theory of Mind, often shortened to ToM, is the ability to understand that other people have their own beliefs, intentions, thoughts, and desires.

Everyone has a mother, but does a child actually need one? In the middle of the twentieth century, scientists thought a toddler's needs were limited to nutrition, comfortable temperature, and other basics. They even thought isolation helped sick children recover in hospitals, so they kept children away from their mothers. It did not take long to discover they were wrong. The British psychiatrist John Bowlby argued in the 1950s that children need love, and that going without affection can lead to serious mental health issues like anxiety and depression in adulthood. Other long-term effects of abandonment include weaker cognitive skills, trouble regulating emotions, violence, and repeating the same unhappy pattern from childhood in a romantic relationship as an adult.

Some children, unfortunately, go through sexual abuse or violence, or they witness those acts, and either path can leave a deep mark on the rest of their lives. The odds of a happy life go up sharply when children grow up with caring parents and stable conditions.

chapters.title: It is inequality, not poverty, that drives violence through broken trust
chapters.text:
People build cultures, but cultures also shape how we behave. By culture, we mean the whole package of beliefs, body image, marriage, music, stories, myths, language, traditions, humor, and ideas about good and evil that set one group of people apart from another. Look at access to resources and opportunity, for example, and life expectancy and literacy look very different in Angola and Iceland. One of the most discussed splits is between collectivist cultures, including many East Asian societies, and individualist cultures, including mainstream American culture. Collectivist cultures lean toward group harmony and intergroup dependence, while individualist cultures lean toward personal uniqueness over group needs. Members of collectivist cultures tend to use first-person plural pronouns, focus on social interactions, and find it easy to take another person's perspective. Those patterns also shape parenting, which in turn shapes how children develop.

The setting where you live also leaves a mark on the brain. Urban residents tend to be wealthier than rural ones, and the amygdala is more reactive to stress in people who live in denser populations. Those same communities have leaned on third-party punishment to keep crime in check, which means they end up being tougher on lawbreakers.

Nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution. That line comes from the biologist Theodosius Dobzhansky.

Kinship ties played a big role in survival. Even today, people keep close relationships with relatives across cultures, and that closeness sometimes nudges us toward irrational decisions. The bias cuts both ways. It lets us empathize with and support people in our own group, but it also makes us easier targets for propaganda that sorts people by religion, race, or nationality, like the targeting of Black people, Roma, and Jewish people. As the earlier chapter on hormones showed, we automatically sort people into Us and Them, and we are quicker to treat out-group members harshly.

Fans of a sports team feel just as much joy when their team wins as they do when their biggest rivals lose.

chapters.title: Things that are not physical can hurt in very real ways
chapters.text:
When the body is hurt, the brain gets the signal from pain receptors, and the regions that handle pain go to work. Those regions collect every detail, the type of pain, the exact spot of the injury, and how strong it feels. Then the brain sorts through the data and lines up possible responses. What happens when someone breaks your heart? The pain is real, but is it different from a physical wound? The neurons in the anterior cingulate cortex treat the two as the same kind of pain. The same region also fires when someone you love is hurt, which is the basis of empathy, the human ability to feel another person's pain.

Recall the earlier note about how we enjoy an enemy's failure. Watching a rival win hurts in a similar way, since the same neurons react to that stress. On the flip side, a little hit of dopamine rewards us when our rivals lose.

Empathy is not the only thing that changes how the body feels and acts. Hunger, certain foods, temperature, and movies all play a role. Some of those cause-and-effect links are obvious. You are less likely to share when you are starving, for example, and a person across from you can seem less emotional if you happen to be holding a cold drink. Other links are less obvious. We can feel physically sick in the middle of an unpleasant situation, and studies suggest that washing your hands afterward can ease the effect and make you feel cleaner inside. Somehow the brain treats moral and physical purity as if they were the same thing. In experiments where people were asked to recall their own bad behavior, they were less likely to act positively afterward, whether they washed their own hands or watched someone else wash theirs. People seem to need to balance out unethical acts, either with physical cleanliness or with acts of kindness that clear the conscience. Metaphors become real in the body.

Metaphor is also a propaganda tool, used to strip enemies of their humanity so that ordinary people are more willing to turn violent against them.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
From a young age, our parents push us to be polite and to put our best foot forward. But human behavior follows its own logic, shaped by a long list of factors. The lessons our parents drill into us are only a small part of the story.

To understand our motives and reactions, we started with the structure of the brain and the amygdala, a key region that helps generate emotional responses. The amygdala plays a role in anxiety, fear, and aggression, and it can react differently to faces from different racial groups. Facts like that, combined with how easily we sort people into Us and Them, show how simple it is to push a person toward hate and violence just by sorting people by race, nationality, or religion.

Sometimes we end up as hostages of the situation. A baby who does not get enough care from the mother in the first years of life is more likely to act in antisocial ways as an adult. Citizens of countries with sharp gender inequality often fall back on stereotypes, like the idea that women are bad at math.

The science gives us general patterns, so it is worth remembering that research findings do not apply to every single case. They describe the majority.

Given how many things can push a person toward violence and skew our judgment, it makes sense to rethink the modern court system so that both victims and suspects get a fair shake.

A few things to try.

Run a small experiment of your own, using the patterns the summary describes. Ask a friend or partner for the same favor when they are hungry and again when they are full, and then compare what happens. Gather some information about the culture you live in. Is it more collectivist or more individualist? Spend a day watching the people around you and see how the split shows up in their behavior. Write down the stereotypes you carry. Which of them could shape how you act, and how could that shape your career or your relationships?
